message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: get_checkpointer creates and registers a new ConnectionPool on every call with no per-conn_str pool reuse — repeated calls (e.g. per study run) leak pools, each holding a live worker thread and a min_size connection
DIMENSION: concurrency & load
SEVERITY: LOW
FILE: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py 183-191, 211-212
DESCRIPTION: The once-per-process dedup (_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS) covers only the schema setup()/advisory-lock bootstrap, not pool creation. Every successful get_checkpointer() call constructs a fresh ConnectionPool (min_size=1, max_size=8) and appends it to _OPEN_POOLS via _register_pool_cleanup, with no lookup/reuse of an existing pool for the same conn_str. Each pool eagerly opens (open=True) and spins up a background maintenance worker thread plus at least one live Postgres connection. If callers invoke configured_checkpointer()/get_checkpointer() repeatedly — for example once per study run under sustained load rather than caching a single checkpointer — the process accumulates an unbounded set of pools, worker threads, and DB connections that are never closed until interpreter exit (_close_all_pools runs only at atexit). This is unbounded resource growth under load (thread and connection exhaustion over a long-lived server process). It is partly mitigated if callers cache the checkpointer, but the factory makes no such guarantee and the dedup structure suggests one-pool-per-conn_str was intended.
SUGGESTED FIX: Cache one open pool per conn_str (e.g. a dict keyed by conn_str guarded by _init_lock) and return a PostgresSaver over the existing pool on subsequent calls, instead of creating and registering a new pool every time. Close/evict cached pools that fail liveness. This bounds threads/connections at one pool per distinct conn_str regardless of call frequency.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate this checkpoint pool reuse claim. Let me read the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Guards the process-local one-time-init bookkeeping. The PG advisory lock serializes DDL
56	# across PROCESSES; this lock makes the in-process check-and-set sequences (atexit
57	# registration, the initialized-conn set) atomic so two threads can't double-register or
58	# double-setup.
59	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
60	
61	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
62	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
63	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
64	_atexit_registered = False
65	
66	# conn_strs whose schema bootstrap (advisory lock + setup() DDL) has already run in this
67	# process — bootstrap once per process per schema; the advisory lock still guards the
68	# first-time cross-process race.
69	_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS: set[str] = set()
70	
71	# Strips ``://[REDACTED]@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
72	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
73	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
74	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[REDACTED]@\s]+@")
75	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=[REDACTED] / ``pgpassword=...``),
76	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
77	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
78	
79	
80	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
81	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
82	
83	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
84	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
85	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
86	    """
87	    try:
88	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
89	        host = u.hostname or ""
90	        port = u.port
91	        scheme = u.scheme
92	        path = u.path
93	    except ValueError:
94	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
95	    if not host:
96	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
97	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
98	        host = f"[{host}]"
99	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
100	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
101	
102	
103	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
104	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
105	
106	    Layers, in order of precision:
107	
108	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
109	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
110	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
111	       *other* URI in the text;
112	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
113	
114	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
115	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
116	    """
117	    if conn_str:
118	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
119	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
120	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
121	
122	
123	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
124	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
125	
126	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
127	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
128	    """
129	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
130	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
131	
132	
133	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
134	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
135	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
136	    that can raise)."""
137	    try:
138	        pool.close()
139	    except Exception:
140	        pass
141	
142	
143	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
144	    for p in _OPEN_POOLS:
145	        _safe_close(p)
146	
147	
148	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
149	    global _atexit_registered
150	    with _init_lock:
151	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
152	        if not _atexit_registered:
153	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
154	            _atexit_registered = True
155	
156	
157	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
158	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
159	
160	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
161	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
162	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
163	    or the extra is not installed.
164	    """
165	    try:
166	        if use_memory:
167	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
168	
169	            return MemorySaver()
170	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
171	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
172	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
173	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
174	        raise RuntimeError(
175	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
176	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
177	        ) from exc
178	
179	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
180	    failure: str | None = None
181	    pool: Any = None
182	    try:
183	        pool = ConnectionPool(
184	            conn_str,
185	            min_size=1,
186	            max_size=8,
187	            kwargs={"autocommit": True, "prepare_threshold": 0, "row_factory": dict_row},
188	            check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
189	            open=True,
190	        )
191	        _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
192	        # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
193	        # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
194	        # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
195	        with _init_lock:
196	            if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS:
197	                with pool.connection() as conn:
198	                    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
199	                    try:
200	                        # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
201	                        # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
202	                        # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
203	                        PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
204	                    finally:
205	                        # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
206	                        # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
207	                        try:
208	                            conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
209	                        except Exception:
210	                            pass
211	                _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS.add(conn_str)
212	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
213	    except Exception as exc:
214	        # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
215	        # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
216	        # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
217	        failure = (
218	            f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
219	            f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
220	            "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
221	        )
222	        # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
223	        # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
224	        if pool is not None:
225	            _safe_close(pool)
226	            with _init_lock:
227	                if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
228	                    _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
229	    raise RuntimeError(failure)
230	
231	
232	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
233	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
234	
235	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
236	    otherwise Postgres.
237	    """
238	    if use_memory is None:
239	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
240	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
241

content.text: The code confirms the assessor's factual description: every `get_checkpointer()` call creates a new `ConnectionPool` (lines 183-191), registers it for cleanup (line 191), and the `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` dedup only short-circuits the schema bootstrap, not pool creation. Let me verify how callers use it - whether the checkpointer is cached or called repeatedly.

content.text: There are no production callers in the engine itself — only tests reference these factories. Let me check the orchestration/graph layer for how the checkpointer is actually wired into a study run.

content.text: The graph layer takes a checkpointer as an injected parameter (`checkpointer: Any | None = None`). It does not call the factory itself. So the factory is invoked by whoever drives the graph compilation. Let me check the test that specifically tests pool behavior across calls (lines 425-495), since pass-1 added a leak fix (#105 referenced in the code).

content.content:
420	            register=lambda fn: state.__setitem__(
421	                "atexit_registered", state["atexit_registered"] + 1
422	            )
423	        ),
424	    )
425	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL", "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay")
426	    return cp, state
427	
428	
429	def test_setup_runs_once_per_conn_and_on_locked_connection(fake_pg):
430	    cp, state = fake_pg
431	    cp.get_checkpointer()
432	    cp.get_checkpointer()  # second call, same conn_str
433	    # setup() ran exactly once despite two factory calls (once-per-conn guard)
434	    assert len(state["setup_on"]) == 1
435	    # the advisory lock and the DDL ran on the SAME connection object
436	    assert state["lock_on"] == state["setup_on"]
437	
438	
439	def test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once(fake_pg):
440	    cp, state = fake_pg
441	    cp.get_checkpointer()
442	    cp.get_checkpointer()
443	    # two pools opened and tracked, but only one shared atexit handler registered
444	    assert len(state["pools"]) == 2
445	    assert len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 2
446	    assert state["atexit_registered"] == 1
447	
448	
449	def test_pool_closed_and_unregistered_when_bootstrap_fails(fake_pg):
450	    # #105: if schema bootstrap fails AFTER the pool opened, the pool must be closed and dropped
451	    # from the cleanup registry — not leaked.
452	    import sys
453

194	def run_study(
195	    plan: StudyPlan,
196	    *,

202	    secret: bytes | None = None,
203	    trail: ProvenanceTrail | None = None,
204	) -> StudyResult:
205	    """Run one study end-to-end through its governed phase sequence. See module docstring.
206	
207	    ``gate_handler`` is **required** (no default), so governance review is a conscious choice —
208	    pass :func:`auto_approve` to opt out of human review explicitly, or an operator handler that
209	    can block/park. ``tracer`` defaults to :class:`OtelTracer` (real spans if OpenTelemetry + a
210	    provider are present, a graceful no-op otherwise). ``tracker`` (optional) logs params/metrics
211	    to an experiment store, best-effort (a tracker failure never aborts the run). ``versioner``
212	    (optional) content-addresses the source so the trail cites retrievable bytes. ``clock``
213	    injects a deterministic clock for the provenance timestamps only (ordering checks always use

215	    mutually exclusive with passing your own ``trail``. ``trail`` may be a caller-owned

`run_study` does not use the LangGraph checkpointer at all — it does not call `compile_graph` or the checkpointer factory. The pipeline study runner and the LangGraph orchestration graph are separate paths. The checkpointer factory has zero production callers in the current codebase; the graph module receives it via dependency injection.

content.text:
Confirmed. The checkpointer is always passed in via DI; there is no production loop calling the factory. The factory is a library-level building block intended to be called once and the result reused (the docstring frames it as "the checkpointer the environment selects").

Now let me assess the technical claim itself. Is the assessor right that each call leaks a pool that's never closed until atexit? Yes — that's exactly what `test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once` enshrines. The mitigating factors:

1. No production caller calls it repeatedly. The intended usage is call-once-and-inject.
2. The leak only manifests if someone writes a per-study-run loop calling the factory — which doesn't exist.
3. `_OPEN_POOLS` grows unboundedly and `_close_all_pools` only runs at atexit — this is a *latent* footgun, not an active leak.

Let me verify the assessor's specific claim that the pool eagerly opens a connection + worker thread (to confirm severity of the per-pool cost) and that there's genuinely no reuse path. The code is clear: `open=True`, `min_size=1`. Each pool does spawn a worker thread and hold ≥1 connection.

Let me write a quick PoC to demonstrate the unbounded growth behavior using the test's fake-pg infrastructure pattern, to make the finding concrete.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc_pool.py <<'PY'
import sys, types
from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp

class FakeConn:
    def execute(self, sql, params=None): pass
class FakePool:
    instances = []
    check_connection = staticmethod(lambda *a, **k: None)
    def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
        self.closed = False
        FakePool.instances.append(self)
    def connection(self):
        c = FakeConn()
        class Ctx:
            def __enter__(s): return c
            def __exit__(s, *a): return False
        return Ctx()
    def close(self): self.closed = True
class FakeSaver:
    def __init__(self, arg): self.arg = arg
    def setup(self): pass

def mod(name, **a):
    m = types.ModuleType(name)
    for k,v in a.items(): setattr(m,k,v)
    sys.modules[name]=m
mod("langgraph"); mod("langgraph.checkpoint")
mod("langgraph.checkpoint.postgres", PostgresSaver=FakeSaver)
mod("langgraph.checkpoint.memory", MemorySaver=type("M",(),{}))
mod("psycopg"); mod("psycopg.rows", dict_row=object())
mod("psycopg_pool", ConnectionPool=FakePool)

import os
os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"]="postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"

N=50
for _ in range(N):
    cp.get_checkpointer()  # same conn_str every time

print("calls:", N)
print("pools created:", len(FakePool.instances))
print("pools registered in _OPEN_POOLS:", len(cp._OPEN_POOLS))
print("pools closed so far:", sum(p.closed for p in FakePool.instances))
print("setup() guard (INITIALIZED set size):", len(cp._INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS))

content.text:
The PoC confirms the mechanism precisely: 50 calls with the identical conn_str create 50 pools, all 50 stay registered in `_OPEN_POOLS`, none are closed, while the schema-bootstrap dedup correctly stays at 1. Each real pool would carry a worker thread + ≥1 live Postgres connection. The factual claim is fully accurate.

Now the production-readiness verdict. The defect is real *in mechanism* but its severity hinges on whether any code calls the factory repeatedly. I confirmed:
- No production code in the repo calls `get_checkpointer`/`configured_checkpointer` in a loop (only tests).
- The graph layer receives the checkpointer via DI (call-once-and-inject is the intended pattern).
- `run_study` (the actual study runner) does not use the LangGraph checkpointer at all.

So today this is a **latent footgun**, not an active leak. The assessor's own description acknowledges it's "partly mitigated if callers cache the checkpointer." The severity recalibration: the assessor said LOW. Given there is no repeated-call site in production code, LOW is appropriate — possibly even bordering on a hardening/design improvement rather than a defect. But it is a genuine production-readiness concern because: (a) the factory is part of the public API (`persistence.__init__` exports it), (b) it makes no caching contract, (c) the dedup structure (`_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` keyed by conn_str) strongly implies one-pool-per-conn_str was the intent, and (d) the natural usage of a "factory" is to call it when you need a checkpointer, which for a long-lived server handling many runs invites exactly this leak.

I'll confirm LOW and is_real=true.

input.reasoning:
VERIFIED TRUE, severity LOW confirmed (latent footgun, not an active leak today).

Mechanism is exactly as the assessor describes and is reproducible. In src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:
- get_checkpointer() unconditionally constructs a fresh ConnectionPool(min_size=1, max_size=8, open=True) on every successful Postgres call (lines 183-191) and appends it to _OPEN_POOLS via _register_pool_cleanup (line 191). There is no dict/lookup keyed by conn_str and no reuse path.
- The only per-conn dedup, _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS (lines 69, 196, 211), guards ONLY the advisory-lock + setup() schema bootstrap, not pool creation. On the 2nd+ call for the same conn_str the bootstrap is skipped but a brand-new pool is still created and returned (line 212).
- Pools are closed only by _close_all_pools at atexit (lines 143-154); within a long-lived process they accumulate without bound. Each psycopg_pool ConnectionPool with open=True eagerly starts a background maintenance worker thread and holds min_size>=1 live Postgres connections, so the growth is in threads + DB connections, not just Python objects.

This is INTENDED current behavior, not an accident: tests/test_persistence.py:439 test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once explicitly asserts two calls => len(state["pools"]) == 2 and len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 2. So the factory deliberately makes one pool per call and offers no caching contract.

Why LOW (not higher) — no active production leak today:
- There is NO production code path that calls get_checkpointer()/configured_checkpointer() repeatedly. Repo-wide grep shows the only callers are tests. The orchestration graph (src/assay_engine/orchestration/graph.py) receives the checkpointer via dependency injection (compile_graph(..., checkpointer=...)) and never constructs it; the actual study runner run_study (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:194) does not use the LangGraph checkpointer at all. So nothing currently loops over the factory per study run.
- The assessor's own report concedes it is "partly mitigated if callers cache the checkpointer."

Why is_real=true despite that: it is a genuine production-readiness defect because the factory is part of the public API (exported from src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py), makes no single-pool guarantee, and the conn_str-keyed dedup structure strongly signals one-pool-per-conn_str was intended. The natural way to use a "checkpointer factory" in a long-lived server that runs many studies is to call it when a checkpointer is needed, which would silently leak a pool/thread/connection per run. The suggested fix (cache one open pool per conn_str under _init_lock, return a PostgresSaver over the existing pool, evict on liveness failure) is correct and low-risk. NOT a pass-1 regression: the #105 failed-bootstrap close/unregister path (lines 222-228) is correct and orthogonal; this gap is about the SUCCESS path.
input.evidence:
PoC (offline, fake psycopg_pool injected, identical conn_str on every call) output:
  calls: 50
  pools created: 50
  pools registered in _OPEN_POOLS: 50
  pools closed so far: 0
  setup() guard (INITIALIZED set size): 1
=> 50 factory calls => 50 pools, all 50 retained in _OPEN_POOLS, zero closed before atexit, while the schema-bootstrap dedup correctly stays at 1. Confirms pool creation is NOT deduped and grows unbounded; only setup() is deduped.

Code: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:183-191 (new ConnectionPool every call, open=True, min_size=1), :191 (_register_pool_cleanup appends, never reuses), :196,211 (_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS guards only bootstrap), :212 (returns saver over the new pool), :143-154 (close only at atexit).

Intended-behavior assertion baking in the leak: tests/test_persistence.py:439-446 test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once asserts len(state["pools"]) == 2 and len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 2 for two calls.

No repeated production caller: grep for get_checkpointer/configured_checkpointer across src/ returns only persistence/checkpoint.py and persistence/__init__.py; graph.py takes checkpointer as an injected param (graph.py:44, :77-78); pipeline.run_study (pipeline.py:194) does not reference the checkpointer.

content.text:
Verification complete. The finding is TRUE but LOW severity.

Summary: The assessor's factual claim is fully accurate and reproducible. In `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py`, `get_checkpointer()` creates a new `ConnectionPool` (lines 183-191) and registers it in `_OPEN_POOLS` (line 191) on every successful call, with no per-conn_str reuse. The `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` dedup covers only the schema bootstrap (setup/advisory-lock), not pool creation. Pools are closed only at atexit.

My PoC (50 calls, same conn_str) confirmed: 50 pools created, all 50 retained in `_OPEN_POOLS`, none closed, while the bootstrap dedup correctly stays at 1. Each real pool carries a worker thread + ≥1 live Postgres connection, so this is unbounded thread/connection growth if the factory is called repeatedly.

Severity stays LOW because there is no production path that calls the factory in a loop: the only callers are tests; `compile_graph` receives the checkpointer via DI; and `run_study` doesn't use the LangGraph checkpointer at all. It is a latent footgun in a public-API factory that makes no caching contract, with a conn_str-keyed dedup structure that signals one-pool-per-conn_str was intended — so it's a genuine production-readiness improvement, not a no-op. Not a pass-1 regression; the #105 failed-bootstrap cleanup path is correct and orthogonal (this gap is on the success path). Suggested fix (cache one open pool per conn_str under `_init_lock`, evict on liveness failure) is sound.
